messages.content:
rephrase 
Apologize for not getting back to you earlier.
I'm sorry for not responding to you sooner.

rephrase
Please note the below regarding our HK  tax briefing with Keith:-
Kindly make a note of the following information regarding our tax briefing about HK with Keith:
again
Take note of the following details concerning the tax briefing about HK with Keith:

rephrase 
Please note the following regarding our HK  tax briefing with Keith
The following information is to be taken into account regarding our tax briefing about HK with Keith.
again
Kindly pay attention to the following details about our HK tax briefing with Keith.
can u rephrase "pay attention" of the above sentence
Could you kindly focus on the following details about our HK tax briefing with Keith.
again
I kindly request you to give your attention to the following information concerning our HK tax briefing with Keith.
how about "Kindly note the following details about our HK tax briefing with Keith.
Yes, "Kindly note the following details about our HK tax briefing with Keith" works perfectly as well.

-	Our briefing was mainly focus on the HK tax implications of his international transfer from Japan to HK
grammar ok?

The grammar in the sentence has a minor issue. Instead of "was mainly focus on," it should be "mainly focused on":

"Our briefing mainly focused on the HK tax implications of his international transfer from Japan to HK."

-	Keith has filed HK salaries tax filings around 10 years ago and has basic understanding of HK salaries tax.
-	He is a US citizen and is a permanent resident of Japan. 
check grammar

The grammar in both sentences looks good. However, for the first sentence, you could adjust it slightly to read:

"Keith filed HK salaries tax filings around 10 years ago and has a basic understanding of HK salaries tax."

-	HK employment start date (1 September 2023) is tentatively correct and would move to HK in early September 2023. 
check grammar

The grammar in the sentence looks good, except for one minor detail: 

Instead of using "tentatively correct," it would be better to use "tentatively confirmed" or "provisionally confirmed" as the start date. 

The updated sentence will be: "The HK employment start date (1 September 2023) is tentatively confirmed, and he would move to HK in early September 2023."
